topic: Recruiter reacts to invisible resumes with real job-description proof
thesis: Turn a timely job-search pain point into one useful long-form teardown and several short proof clips.

sourceNotes.title: YouTube Partner Program eligibility
sourceNotes.note: Use YouTube as a monetization target, but optimize first for site conversions.

sourceNotes.title: Pew Research Center Social Media Fact Sheet
sourceNotes.note: YouTube, Instagram, and TikTok are large discovery channels for job-search education.

youtube.title: This Resume Looks Fine But Stays Invisible
youtube.seoTitle: This Resume Looks Fine But Stays Invisible | Resume Teardown
youtube.description: A recruiter-style resume teardown showing how a qualified demand generation resume can stay invisible when it misses job-description proof, then fixing one bullet without adding fake experience.

scriptSections.label: Cold open
scriptSections.script: This resume looks professional, and that is the trap. The job description screams HubSpot, CAC, LinkedIn Ads, and lifecycle marketing. The resume whispers helped with campaigns. That is invisible experience.
scriptSections.visual: Open on polished resume beside job description; red stamp says professional fog, then Signal points at the missing terms.

scriptSections.label: Open loop
scriptSections.script: In the next few minutes, we are going to do this like a recruiter would actually see it. First, I will pull the clues out of the job description. Then I will run the resume through a simple search test. Then we will find the mistake that makes a qualified person look generic. Finally, we will fix one bullet live and watch the Signal score move from 34 to 92. The point is not to make the candidate sound louder. The point is to make the existing proof easier to find.
scriptSections.visual: Timeline cards: mistake, search test, fix, score reveal; keep the resume/JD split screen visible.

scriptSections.label: Translate the job description
scriptSections.script: Start with the job description, not the resume. A Demand Generation Manager posting is usually not asking for vague marketing energy. It is asking for pipeline sourced, HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis, lifecycle marketing, and MQL-to-SQL conversion. Those are not magic keywords. They are clues about what the hiring team wants to see quickly. If your resume has the experience but calls everything campaigns, support, and coordination, you are making the reviewer translate your background for you. Reviewers are not mind readers, and they are usually moving fast.
scriptSections.visual: Keyword extraction board pulls terms from the JD into bright proof chips.

scriptSections.label: Recruiter search test
scriptSections.script: Now pretend I am searching resumes. I type HubSpot. I type CAC. I type LinkedIn Ads. I type pipeline. If this candidate has done that work, the resume should give me a clean match. Right now, it barely does. That does not mean the person is bad. It means the resume is asking me to infer too much. This is where a lot of job seekers lose attention. They think the resume is being humble and professional. To a recruiter, it can read like polite fog.
scriptSections.visual: Recruiter search box types each keyword; weak resume returns partial or empty matches.

scriptSections.label: Resume crime scene
scriptSections.script: Here is the resume crime scene: helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. That is not bad experience. It is just under-labeled experience. The bullet has HubSpot, which is useful, but it hides scope, audience, and business result. What kind of lists? For what campaigns? What happened because of the work? The evidence is wearing camouflage. A stronger resume does not need to brag. It needs to remove the camouflage so the proof is visible in a six-second skim.
scriptSections.visual: Circle the weak bullet; Signal drops a red evidence marker beside it.

scriptSections.label: Why it gets missed
scriptSections.script: This is not about tricking software. The ATS is not a wizard sitting in a basement rejecting good people for sport. The practical problem is simpler: resumes get stored, searched, skimmed, compared, and forgotten. When the job description says CAC analysis and your resume says helped with campaigns, the strongest version of your experience is not on the page yet. It is stuck in your memory. A hiring team should not have to interview you just to discover the basic proof that belonged in the bullet.
scriptSections.visual: Before bullet fades under fog overlay while JD terms stay sharp on the right.

scriptSections.label: Live bullet fix
scriptSections.script: Now we translate, not fabricate. The weak bullet says: helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. A stronger version says: built HubSpot lead-scoring and LinkedIn retargeting segments that cut CAC 32% and lifted MQL-to-SQL conversion 18%. Same work category. Same candidate. Much clearer proof. Notice what changed. We added the tool, the channel, the business metric, and the result. We did not add a fake job, a fake degree, or a fake skill. That is the line Signal has to protect.
scriptSections.visual: Before bullet slides out; after bullet locks in with HubSpot, LinkedIn Ads, CAC, and 32% highlighted.

scriptSections.label: Score reveal
scriptSections.script: Now the score jump makes sense. Before, the resume was sitting at 34 out of 100 because the job description language and the resume proof were not connected. After the rewrite, the example moves to 92 out of 100 because the same experience is mapped to HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis, lifecycle marketing, pipeline, and conversion proof. The value is not the number by itself. The value is knowing exactly what changed, and why the new version is easier for a human to understand.
scriptSections.visual: Score meter animates 34 to 92; missing keyword chips flip from red to green.

scriptSections.label: Trust and CTA
scriptSections.script: That is the whole Signal promise. Find the real proof already in your work. Make it match the job description. Keep it honest. No fake jobs, no fake tools, no guaranteed outcomes, and no panic about mythical resume robots. If you are about to apply, paste the job description first and check your free Signal score. If the gap is real, then paying for a targeted resume and cover letter package makes sense. If the gap is not real, you just learned that before spending money.
scriptSections.visual: Signal score page, trust badges, no fake experience note, and free score CTA.

youtube.cta: Paste the job description and check your free Signal score before you apply.

shorts.title: This marketing resume hid the actual revenue proof
shorts.hook: This marketing resume sounds busy. Recruiters need revenue proof.
shorts.script: This marketing resume sounds busy. Recruiters need revenue proof. Target: Demand Generation Manager. JD asks for HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis. Corporate weather report: high synergy, zero proof. Resume says: Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. Real, but buried. Better: built lead scoring and retargeting that cut CAC 32 percent. Same person, clearer proof. 34 to 92. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Open on the weak Marketing Coordinator -> Demand Gen Candidate with the score at 34/100.
Show the target role: Demand Generation Manager.
Highlight missing terms: HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis.
Circle the weak bullet: Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails.
Signal points at the missing proof instead of taking over the scene.
Reveal the rewrite: Built HubSpot lead-scoring and LinkedIn retargeting segments that cut CAC 32% and lifted MQL-to-SQL conversion 18%.
Score jumps to 92/100.
End on the free Signal score CTA with UTM-ready posting metadata.

props.subline: AI polish can hide the actual evidence.
props.voiceover_text: This marketing resume sounds busy. Recruiters need revenue proof. Target: Demand Generation Manager. JD asks for HubSpot workflows, LinkedIn Ads, CAC analysis. Corporate weather report: high synergy, zero proof. Resume says: Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails. Real, but buried. Better: built lead scoring and retargeting that cut CAC 32 percent. Same person, clearer proof. 34 to 92. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
weakBullets: Supported email and social campaigns across multiple channels.
Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails.
Coordinated paid social assets with design and sales.
props.beforeBullet: Helped maintain HubSpot lists for webinars and nurture emails.
props.afterBullet: Built HubSpot lead-scoring and LinkedIn retargeting segments that cut CAC 32% and lifted MQL-to-SQL conversion 18%.
props.problemPunchline: A recruiter cannot search for proof you buried.

shorts.title: This sales resume forgot to say sales
shorts.hook: This sales resume has conversations. The job needs pipeline.
shorts.script: This sales resume has conversations. The job needs pipeline. Target: Mid-Market Account Executive. JD asks for Salesforce, pipeline generation, quota attainment. Pipeline proof is hiding under a hoodie. Resume says: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory. Real, but buried. Better: generated 1.8 million in qualified Salesforce pipeline. Same person, clearer proof. 37 to 88. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Open on the weak Account Executive Resume with the score at 37/100.
Show the target role: Mid-Market Account Executive.
Highlight missing terms: Salesforce, pipeline generation, quota attainment.
Circle the weak bullet: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory.
Signal points at the missing proof instead of taking over the scene.
Reveal the rewrite: Generated $1.8M in qualified Salesforce pipeline through 42 monthly discovery calls and MEDDICC-based account prioritization.
Score jumps to 88/100.
End on the free Signal score CTA with UTM-ready posting metadata.

props.subline: Recruiters search for role language and proof.
props.voiceover_text: This sales resume has conversations. The job needs pipeline. Target: Mid-Market Account Executive. JD asks for Salesforce, pipeline generation, quota attainment. Pipeline proof is hiding under a hoodie. Resume says: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory. Real, but buried. Better: generated 1.8 million in qualified Salesforce pipeline. Same person, clearer proof. 37 to 88. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
weakBullets: Managed customer conversations and followed up with prospects.
Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory.
Worked with marketing and customer success on accounts.
props.beforeBullet: Responsible for sales opportunities in assigned territory.
props.afterBullet: Generated $1.8M in qualified Salesforce pipeline through 42 monthly discovery calls and MEDDICC-based account prioritization.
props.problemPunchline: Sales resumes need numbers faster than adjectives.

shorts.title: This developer resume hides the stack
shorts.hook: This developer resume says frontend. The job searches React.
shorts.script: This developer resume says frontend. The job searches React. Target: Frontend Software Engineer. JD asks for React, TypeScript, Next.js. The tech stack is wearing a fake mustache. Resume says: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools. Real, but buried. Better: shipped React and TypeScript checkout work that reduced drop-off 14 percent. Same person, clearer proof. 41 to 90. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
storyboard: Open on the weak Frontend Engineer Resume with the score at 41/100.
Show the target role: Frontend Software Engineer.
Highlight missing terms: React, TypeScript, Next.js.
Circle the weak bullet: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools.
Signal points at the missing proof instead of taking over the scene.
Reveal the rewrite: Shipped React and TypeScript checkout components in Next.js, reducing form drop-off 14% and closing 11 accessibility issues.
Score jumps to 90/100.
End on the free Signal score CTA with UTM-ready posting metadata.

props.subline: The fix is translation, not fabrication.
props.voiceover_text: This developer resume says frontend. The job searches React. Target: Frontend Software Engineer. JD asks for React, TypeScript, Next.js. The tech stack is wearing a fake mustache. Resume says: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools. Real, but buried. Better: shipped React and TypeScript checkout work that reduced drop-off 14 percent. Same person, clearer proof. 41 to 90. Check your free Signal score before you apply.
weakBullets: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools.
Collaborated with designers and backend engineers.
Helped improve application performance and user experience.
props.beforeBullet: Worked on frontend features for internal and customer-facing tools.
props.afterBullet: Shipped React and TypeScript checkout components in Next.js, reducing form drop-off 14% and closing 11 accessibility issues.
props.problemPunchline: Engineering resumes need proof of stack plus outcome.

monetization.primaryGoal: Site revenue from resume / cover letter packages, not ad revenue.
monetization.cta: Free Signal score -> $9.99 resume or $14.99 bundle.
monetization.upsell: Promote bundle after score completion and multi-role pack after first purchase.

viewerCustomerReview.viewerEmotion: Frustrated job hunters should feel called out, amused, and relieved rather than shamed.
viewerCustomerReview.customerBridge: Laugh at the vague bullet, then show the free score as the low-risk next step.
entertainmentRules: Open with a frustrated job-hunter joke or painfully relatable line in the first 2 seconds.
Make the viewer laugh before teaching: rage scrolling, spreadsheet of applications, 'professional yap' bullets, or AI resume sameness.
Keep the resume/job description on screen as the main character; Signal mascot is the mischievous guide, not a corporate presenter.
Use blunt recruiter-reacts energy: roast the bullet, then rescue the person.
Every joke must punch at the broken job-search process or vague resume language, never at unemployed people.
End with a calm paid bridge: free score first, bundle only after the gap is obvious.

notes: Add one job-search-safe joke or visual gag.
